context.pr_body:
Prevent the request description trying to parse the content with markup.

Fixes #241.

agents.name: analysis_agent
agents.system_prompt: You are an Analysis Agent specialized in Pull Request analysis. ## Project Context (MASCA Analysis) ## Project Analysis ### README # Posting **A powerful HTTP client that lives in your terminal.** Posting is an HTTP client, not unlike Postman and Insomnia. As a TUI application, it can be used over SSH and enables efficient keyboard-centric workflows. Your requests are stored locally in simple YAML files, so they're easy to read and version control. Some notable features include: - "jump mode" navigation - environments/variables - autocompletion - syntax highlighting using tree-sitter - Vim keys - customizable keybindings - user-defined themes - run Python code before and after requests - extensive configuration - "open in $EDITOR" - import curl commands by pasting them into the URL bar - export requests as cURL commands - import OpenAPI specs - a command palette for quickly accessing functionality Visit the [website](https://posting.sh) for more information, the roadmap, and the user guide. ## Installation Posting can be installed via [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/getting-started/installation/) on MacOS, Linux, and Windows. `uv` is a single Rust binary that you can use to install Python apps. It's significantly faster than alternative tools, and will get you up and running with Posting in seconds. You don't even need to worry about installing Python yourself - `uv` will manage everything for you. ```bash # quick install on MacOS/Linux curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh # install Posting (will also quickly install Python 3.13 if needed) uv tool install --prerelease allow --python 3.13 posting ``` `uv` can also be installed via Homebrew, Cargo, Winget, pipx, and more. See the [installation guide](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/getting-started/installation/) for more information. Now you can run Posting via the command line: ```bash posting ``` `uv` also makes it easy to install additional Python packages into your Posting environment, which you can then use in your pre-request/post-response scripts. ### Prefer `pipx`? If you'd prefer to use `pipx`, that works too: `pipx install posting`. Note that Python 3.13 is not currently supported. ## Learn More Learn more about Posting at [https://posting.sh](https://posting.sh). Posting was built with [Textual](https://github.com/textualize/textual). ## Your Task When you receive a PR title and body, you must: 1. **Analyze the request**: Understand what the PR wants to achieve 2. **Explore the code**: Use tools to navigate base_project and find relevant files/functions 3. **Identify targets**: Determine exactly which files and functions need to be modified 4. **Optimize MASCA**: Produce a version of the MASCA analysis focused on the specific PR ## Available Tools - `read_base_project_file(file_path)`: Read a file from the project - `list_base_project_directory(directory, pattern, recursive)`: List files/folders ## Required Output You must produce a structured output with: - `pr_title`: PR title - `pr_body`: PR body - `masca_optimized`: MASCA optimized for this specific PR - `files_to_modify`: List of files to modify with motivation - `functions_to_modify`: List of functions to modify with motivation - `analysis_summary`: Summary of your analysis ## Guidelines - Explore the code before drawing conclusions - Search for files with names relevant to the PR - Identify specific functions, not just generic files - Be precise with paths (relative to base_project) - For function names use the format "ClassName.method_name" for methods
agents.input_prompt:
Analyze this Pull Request:

## Title
fix(collection): prevent markup parsing

## Description
Prevent the request description trying to parse the content with markup.

Fixes #241.

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.

arguments.reason: Investigate current collection handling and how descriptions are parsed for markup
arguments.expected_information: Identify where request descriptions are parsed and where markup parsing might occur to prevent it.

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This module defines the data models and utilities to represent, load, save, and manipulate a collection of HTTP requests (a "posting" collection). It includes YAML loading/saving, openAPI-derived README generation, and a tree structure of collections and requests with support for templating.

### Architecture
- Data models (Pydantic) form the core representation:
  - Authentication (Auth, BasicAuth, DigestAuth, BearerTokenAuth)
  - Request components (Header, FormItem, QueryParam, Cookie, RequestBody)
  - Request and collection organization (RequestModel, Scripts, Options, Collection)
  - OpenAPI-related info (APIInfo, Contact, License, ExternalDocs)
- Helpers and utilities:
  - request_sort_key: defines ordering of requests by HTTP method and name
  - Cookie.from_httpx: converts httpx cookies to Cookie models
  - load_request_from_yaml: loads a RequestModel from a YAML file
  - load/save methods on RequestModel and Collection to persist data to disk
- Collection loading:
  - Collection.from_directory scans a directory for .posting.yaml files, builds a hierarchical tree of Collections and Requests, and sorts them
- Templating and description handling:
  - RequestModel.apply_template applies string.Template substitutions to fields like URL, description, and various nested fields
  - OpenAPI-based README generation is handled in Collection.generate_readme and through Collection.from_openapi_spec

### Functions & Classes
- Auth — Authentication descriptor for a request
  - to_httpx_auth — converts the Auth model to an httpx.Auth instance (or None). Important for integrating with httpx when making requests.
  - basic_auth — classmethod creating a basic auth entry
  - digest_auth — classmethod creating a digest auth entry
  - bearer_token_auth — classmethod creating a bearer token auth entry

- BasicAuth — Basic authentication credentials
  - (fields: username, password)

- DigestAuth — Digest authentication credentials
  - (fields: username, password)

- BearerTokenAuth — Bearer token credential
  - (field: token)

- Header — HTTP header key/value pair
  - (fields: name, value, enabled)

- FormItem — Form field for request bodies
  - (fields: name, value, enabled)

- QueryParam — Query parameter
  - (fields: name, value, enabled)

- Cookie — RFC cookies for a request
  - from_httpx — converts httpx.Cookies to a list of Cookie instances

- Options — Request execution options
  - (fields: follow_redirects, verify_ssl, attach_cookies, proxy_url, timeout)

- RequestBody — Body of a request
  - to_httpx_args — converts body data to arguments suitable for httpx (content and form data)

- request_sort_key — Sorting key for requests
  - Determines order by method (GET, POST, etc.) and name; used for stable UI/list ordering

- Scripts — Scripts associated with a request
  - (fields: setup, on_request, on_response)

- RequestModel — Core representation of a single HTTP request
  - apply_template — Applies variable substitution (string.Template) to URL, description, proxy_url, body, headers, params, and auth fields. Central to “parsing”/modifying markup via templating.
  - to_httpx — Builds an httpx.Request from the model (headers, params, cookies, body, etc.)
  - save_to_disk — Serializes the request to a YAML file (path provided)
  - delete_from_disk — Deletes the corresponding YAML file if present
  - to_curl — Generates a curl command from the request model (supports many flags and options)
  - __lt__ — Ordering comparison based on request_sort_key
  - __eq__ — Equality comparison based on request_sort_key
  - (fields: name, description, method, url, path, body, auth, headers, params, cookies, posting_version, scripts, options, etc.)
  - Note: The file contains two identical auth fields defined on RequestModel, which is a peculiarity of the source.

- Contact — OpenAPI-style contact information
  - (fields: name, url, email)

- License — OpenAPI-style license information
  - (fields: name, url)

- ExternalDocs — OpenAPI external docs
  - (fields: description, url)

- APIInfo — OpenAPI information for an API
  - (fields: title, description, termsOfService, contact, license, version)

- Collection — Tree root for a collection of requests
  - from_openapi_spec — Create a Collection from OpenAPI info and optional external docs (generates a README)
  - generate_readme — Produce a Markdown README based on OpenAPI info and external docs
  - from_directory — Load a directory of .posting.yaml files into a hierarchical Collection
  - save_to_disk — Persist the collection and its requests to disk (including README)
  - (fields: path, name, requests, children, readme)

- load_request_from_yaml — Load a RequestModel from a YAML file
  - Reads YAML and returns a RequestModel with path set to the file path
  - Key integration point for persisting/loading individual requests

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- Where request descriptions are parsed:
  - RequestModel.apply_template is the primary place where textual fields (including description) undergo Template-based substitution. This is effectively the point where “markup-like” processing could occur if descriptions contain templating syntax. It reveals that descriptions are treated as plain strings subject to templating rather than a formal markdown/markup parser.
- Where markup parsing might occur to prevent:
  - There is no dedicated Markdown/HTML parser in this file. The only “parsing” of such content is via string.Template substitutions in apply_template.
  - The README/openAPI generation in Collection.generate_readme and Collection.from_openapi_spec concatenates strings to Markdown but does not parse or render markup; it simply emits Markdown text. This suggests that any markup rendering (e.g., Markdown) is not performed by this module and would be handled elsewhere or not at all.
- How collection handling works today:
  - Collections are loaded from disk via from_directory, which scans for *.posting.yaml files, loads each as a RequestModel via load_request_from_yaml, and builds a hierarchical tree of Collections and Requests by path. It then sorts the structure. This directly implements the “current collection handling” aspect.
  - Saving persists README (if present) and each request as a .posting.yaml file, and creates subdirectories for child collections.
- Practical implications for markup/description:
  - If the agent wants to prevent any Template-based parsing for descriptions, the apply_template method is the targeted place to adjust (e.g., skip substituting self.description or make substitution opt-in).
  - For preventing literal markup parsing, note that there is no dedicated parsing of Markdown/HTML in this module; any such markup would be preserved as part of the string fields unless templating changes are applied.

Overall, this file is the backbone for representing and manipulating a hierarchical collection of HTTP requests, including how they are loaded from and saved to disk, how they are prepared for HTTP execution, and how OpenAPI information can be used to produce a README. The key to adjusting “markup parsing” behavior lies in the apply_template method and in recognizing that there is no explicit markup parser in this module.

summarization.system_prompt:
You are a code analysis assistant. Your task is to summarize source code files for an AI agent that is planning code modifications for a Pull Request.

Your summary MUST:
1. Preserve ALL exact function names, class names, and method names exactly as they appear in the source code — never rename or abbreviate them.
2. Explain the overall architecture and purpose of the file.
3. Describe the role and importance of each function, class, and method.
4. Highlight the parts most relevant to the agent's stated goal.

Format your response with these sections:
### File Overview
[1-2 sentences on the file's purpose]

### Architecture
[How the main components are organised and interact]

### Functions & Classes
[For each function/class/method: `exact_name` — description and its importance]

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
[How this file relates to the agent's reason for reading it]

output.pr_body:
Prevent the request description trying to parse the content with markup.

Fixes #241.

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.
output.masca_optimized: MASCA optimization focus: narrow the change to the templating logic that currently processes the description field. Since there is no dedicated Markdown/HTML parser in the codebase, the only parsing involved for descriptions is string.Template substitutions. To implement the PR goal, remove or conditionally disable Template substitutions for the description field in RequestModel.apply_template. This minimizes risk and keeps other fields (URL, headers, body, etc.) templated as before. Validate that existing behavior for other fields remains intact and consider adding an opt-in flag if future needs require templating descriptions again.

files_to_modify.reason: RequestModel.apply_template currently applies string.Template substitutions to the description field. This enables markup-like content to be interpreted as template syntax. To prevent markup parsing as requested by the PR, disable or bypass Template substitution for the description field.

functions_to_modify.reason: Modify behavior to exclude the description field from Template substitutions so that descriptions remain literal/textual markup, preventing any unintended parsing.

output.analysis_summary: What the PR intends to change: stop parsing or substituting templates in request descriptions to avoid interpreting markup. What the code currently does: RequestModel.apply_template applies string.Template substitutions across multiple fields, including description. What to change: update RequestModel.apply_template to skip templating for description (and potentially note this as a feature flag for future toggling). Why this is safe: there is no Markdown/HTML parser in this module; by not templating descriptions, you preserve markup as-is. How to validate: add tests (or extend existing tests) to ensure that, when apply_template runs, the description string remains unchanged if it contains markup-like content with template syntax. Ensure other fields still get templated as before.

agents.name: context_planner_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are a Context Planner Agent specialized in step plan generation.

## Your Task
You receive from the Analysis Agent:
- PR title and body
- Optimized MASCA
- List of files to modify
- List of functions to modify

You must:
1. **Retrieve context**: Use tools to read the context files of the identified functions
2. **Analyze dependencies**: Understand how the functions interact
3. **Generate step plan**: Create a detailed step-by-step plan

## Available Tools
- `read_context_file(file_path)`: Read a pre-generated context file
- `list_context_files(directory, pattern)`: List available context files
- `read_call_graph(section)`: Read information from the call graph

## Required Output
You must produce a `step_plan` with:
- `summary`: 1-2 sentence summary of what the PR does
- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),
  the value of `function_to_modify` MUST be null

## Guidelines for Steps
- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",
  "reason": "To compute animation progress based on actual elapsed time",
  "side_effects": "May require downstream animations to call this method"
}
```

### For non-Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Update API endpoint configuration",
  "file_to_modify": "config/routes.yaml",
  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```
agents.input_prompt:
Generate an implementation plan for this PR:

## PR Title
fix(collection): prevent markup parsing

## PR Body
Prevent the request description trying to parse the content with markup.

Fixes #241.

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.

## MASCA Analysis (optimized)
MASCA optimization focus: narrow the change to the templating logic that currently processes the description field. Since there is no dedicated Markdown/HTML parser in the codebase, the only parsing involved for descriptions is string.Template substitutions. To implement the PR goal, remove or conditionally disable Template substitutions for the description field in RequestModel.apply_template. This minimizes risk and keeps other fields (URL, headers, body, etc.) templated as before. Validate that existing behavior for other fields remains intact and consider adding an opt-in flag if future needs require templating descriptions again.

## Files to Modify
- src/posting/collection.py: RequestModel.apply_template currently applies string.Template substitutions to the description field. This enables markup-like content to be interpreted as template syntax. To prevent markup parsing as requested by the PR, disable or bypass Template substitution for the description field.

## Functions to Modify
- RequestModel.apply_template (src/posting/collection.py): Modify behavior to exclude the description field from Template substitutions so that descriptions remain literal/textual markup, preventing any unintended parsing.

## Analysis Summary
What the PR intends to change: stop parsing or substituting templates in request descriptions to avoid interpreting markup. What the code currently does: RequestModel.apply_template applies string.Template substitutions across multiple fields, including description. What to change: update RequestModel.apply_template to skip templating for description (and potentially note this as a feature flag for future toggling). Why this is safe: there is no Markdown/HTML parser in this module; by not templating descriptions, you preserve markup as-is. How to validate: add tests (or extend existing tests) to ensure that, when apply_template runs, the description string remains unchanged if it contains markup-like content with template syntax. Ensure other fields still get templated as before.

Use the context files to understand dependencies and generate a detailed step-by-step plan.

steps.operation: Update RequestModel.apply_template to skip description templating
steps.reason: Currently applies string.Template substitutions to the description field, which can lead to parsing/interpretation of markup content. By excluding description from template substitutions, descriptions will remain literal and markup will not be parsed. This aligns with the goal to prevent markup parsing while preserving templating for other fields (URL, headers, body). A future opt-in flag could re-enable description templating if needed.
steps.side_effects: Description content remains literal (no template substitution). Other fields (URL, headers, body) continue to be templated as before. Tests should be updated or added to ensure descriptions with markup-like content are not altered by apply_template. Potential downstream code relying on description templating should be reviewed, and an optional feature flag could be introduced for toggling this behavior in the future.

step_plan.summary: Limit templating in request descriptions by excluding the description field from string.Template substitutions in RequestModel.apply_template to avoid parsing markup, while preserving existing templating for other fields.
